title: Code modules initiative | Stats NZ
content:
## Code modules initiative
The Code Modules Initiative is an all-of-data system effort to make New Zealand’s integrated data assets easier to use, and understandable for everyone, including non-technical people.

## What you’ll find on this page
What are Code Modules Available code modules and where to find them How to use a code module in your research Upcoming code modules

## What are Code Modules
Following a successful pilot in 2022, the Code Modules Initiative has attracted the backing of eight agencies and non-government users, who see value in creating data tools, called “code modules,” that contain high-quality code and documentation for foundational measures needed by researchers – such as benefit receipt, migration status, highest qualification, employment status, and total income.
These modules capture the knowledge of people from across the data system to provide both the code to create these measures, and the information needed to use them safely.
They are maintained and kept up-to-date along with the source data, to ensure researchers can consistently and accurately report on these concepts over time, and create the same figures no matter what agency or organisation they work for.

## Available code modules and where to find them
The table below lists the modules available for researchers in the Data Lab and links to the Integrated Data Commons. Each module includes code, documentation, and links to references.
The Integrated Data Commons is a secure online community where integrated data researchers and stakeholders can come together to ask questions, share expertise, engage in discussions and collaborate. To learn more about the Commons, you can email admins.idcommons@stats.govt.nz .
ASH and PAH module (Sunsetting)
This module defines spells where clients have had a publicly funded hospital event which is considered to be an Ambulatory Sensitive Care Hospitalisation (ASH) event or Potentially Avoidable Hospitalisation (PAH). This includes the diagnosis (ICD10 code) and the relevant ages for which it is considered an ASH/PAH event.
[moh_clean].[pub_fund_hosp_discharges_diag] [moh_clean].[pub_fund_hosp_discharges_event] [moh_ASH_PAH_lookup]
This module defines spells where clients have had a publicly funded hospital event that is classified as an Ambulatory Sensitive Care Hospitalisation (ASH) event. This includes the ICD10 diagnosis code and the relevant ages for which it is considered an ASH event.
[{targetprefix}_ASH]
[moh_clean].[pub_fund_hosp_discharges_diag] [moh_clean].[pub_fund_hosp_discharges_event] [moh_ASH_lookup]
Business Profile Modules
This module creates a set of 17 tables and views of permanent enterprises with basic characteristic information such as age, geographical unit, type, industry, size by sales or revenue and size by labour input. This module has been informed by the Fabling and Maré Motu working papers.

Driver licence endorsements
This module creates a table of spells by the endorsement type (such as passenger, driver instructor etc) and endorsement status (such as Current, Cancelled etc). It is sourced from Waka Kotahi NZ Transport Agency’s wider register of driver licences.
NZTA_DLR_ENDORSEMENT
[nzta_clean].[drivers_licence_register]
Driver licence status
This module creates tables of when individuals held a driver’s licence by vehicle class and stage (learners to full) and status (current, suspended, expired).
NZTA_DRIVER_LICENCES_STATUS
[nzta_clean].[drivers_licence_register] [security].[concordance]
Educational attainment
This module creates 11 tables detailing different education qualifications. They are summarised in a final table which creates the highest NZQF level spells for individuals and the time period for which an NZQF level gained was the highest ever achieved by the person until the date it gets superseded by a higher NZQF.

Ethnic Communities' groupings
Builds a table of ethnicity groupings relevant to the Ministry for Ethnic Communities such as MELAA+, Continental European, Chinese and Indian. The table uses similar business rules to the Personal Details table and shows ethnicity information for the top ranked data source for each [snz_uid].

Family violence sexual violence indicator
The module construct spells of people who have perpetrated or been impacted by family violence and sexual violence in NZ. These are based on recorded interactions with Oranga Tamariki, Ministry of Health, ACC, Police or Ministry of Justice.

Intellectual (learning) disability
Creates a table of individuals with a recorded diagnosis of intellectual (learning) disability based on interaction with health services or other agencies, as well as tables of individuals for nine other conditions: ADHD, Autism, Cerebral Palsy, Developmental Delay, Down Syndrome, FASD, Fragile X Syndrome, Klinefelter Syndrome, and Spina Bifida.

Oranga Tamariki – Assessment of Investigation and Substantiated Abuse Findings
The Oranga Tamariki Assessment/Investigation and Substantiated Abuse Finding module creates tables of Child Family Assessments (CFA)/Investigations and Substantiated Abuse Findings by Care and Protection system. CFAs/Investigations and Abuse Findings are critical components of child protection system and helps support and consistencies about understanding of Children at risk.

Secondary specialist mental health and addiction
This module identifies secondary specialist mental health services (activities) accessed by individuals in New Zealand. These contacts can be used to produce estimated counts of secondary specialist mental health and addiction service uses (activities) of people utilising services, estimated counts of people utilising services by provider type (district/NGO), and the type of service that was provided (contact, face-to-face contact, addiction service vs mental health service, bednights).

Victimisation events
The purpose of this module is to construct Victimisation events experienced by individuals in New Zealand. These events can be used to produce estimated counts of people who are victimised, the date the victimisation occurred, the date the victimisation was reported to Police, the type of victimisation that occurred, and additional event information.
victimisation_events
[IDI_Clean].[pol_clean].[nia_links] [IDI_Clean].[pol_clean].[post_count_victimisations] [IDI_Clean].[pol_clean].[pre_count_victimisations] [IDI_Clean].[security].[spine_population]

## How to use a code module in your research
If your Data Lab project has access to the underlying data used to create the module, we try to automatically grant you access to the module output table and the associated code and documentation within the Data Lab.
To check if you should have access to a module, go to the Commons and find the module you are interested in. In the "Dependencies section, there are a list of source tables for that module – in almost all cases, you will need to have access to all of these source tables to get module access. If you don’t have access to the some of the source tables, we recommend you request access to them as a first step.
If you think you have access to all the underlying data, but don't have access to the module, please submit a project variation application to get this resolved. In the application, you need only list the name of the module you’re requesting and the module output tables (e.g. [MSD_EMP_ASSISTANCE]) in the section for adding a new dataset (Part 4). You do not need to list a reason for requesting access.

## Upcoming code modules
Module deployment can occur at any time, but most modules are expected to be deployed or re-deployed as part of the triannual refresh cycle. The modules that are currently in development are:
- Corrections management spells
- Interactions with Police

## Learn more about code modules
To learn more about our code modules initiative, email Eric.KrassoiPeach@stats.govt.nz .
Page updated 17 July 2025.
